subject: Torts
coverage_standard: Expanded NCBE-aligned Torts rule bank built to track the MBE Torts outline categories: intentional torts, negligence, strict and products liability, nuisance, defamation/privacy, misrepresentation, and intentional interference with business relations

rules.title: Intent
rules.prompt_question: What intent is required for an intentional tort?
rules.rule_statement: Intent exists when the defendant acts with the purpose to cause the consequences of the act or knows with substantial certainty that those consequences will result.
rules.application_example: If a defendant swings at another person intending to strike, intent is satisfied even if the resulting injury is more serious than expected.
rules.common_trap: Intent to act alone is not enough. The defendant must intend the relevant consequence or know it is substantially certain.

rules.title: Transferred Intent
rules.prompt_question: When does transferred intent apply?
rules.rule_statement: Transferred intent applies when the defendant intends to commit battery, assault, false imprisonment, trespass to land, or trespass to chattels against one person or property but instead commits one of those torts against another.
rules.application_example: If a defendant throws a rock intending to hit A but hits B, intent transfers.
rules.common_trap: Transferred intent traditionally applies only among the five old writ torts, not to IIED or conversion.

rules.title: Battery
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of battery?
rules.rule_statement: Battery requires an intentional act that causes harmful or offensive contact with the plaintiff's person.
rules.application_example: Spitting on someone is battery because it is offensive contact.
rules.common_trap: The plaintiff need not be aware of the contact at the time it occurs.

rules.title: Contact with Plaintiff's Person
rules.prompt_question: What counts as contact with the plaintiff's person for battery?
rules.rule_statement: Battery includes contact with anything closely connected to the plaintiff's body, such as clothing, a plate in the plaintiff's hand, or an object the plaintiff is holding.
rules.application_example: Knocking a tray out of the plaintiff's hand may constitute battery.
rules.common_trap: Direct skin-to-skin touching is not required.

rules.title: Assault
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of assault?
rules.rule_statement: Assault requires an intentional act causing the plaintiff reasonable apprehension of imminent harmful or offensive contact.
rules.application_example: Pointing a fist at someone and stepping toward them may be assault if the plaintiff reasonably expects immediate contact.
rules.common_trap: Words alone are usually insufficient unless coupled with conduct or surrounding circumstances creating imminence.

rules.title: Apparent Ability
rules.prompt_question: Why does apparent ability matter in assault?
rules.rule_statement: Assault requires that the plaintiff reasonably perceive that the defendant has the apparent present ability to carry out the threatened contact.
rules.application_example: A threat made from too far away to be carried out immediately usually is not assault.
rules.common_trap: Future threats are not imminent.

rules.title: False Imprisonment
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of false imprisonment?
rules.rule_statement: False imprisonment requires an intentional act that confines the plaintiff to a bounded area without lawful privilege and against the plaintiff's will, and the plaintiff is aware of the confinement or harmed by it.
rules.application_example: Locking a customer in a back room without justification may be false imprisonment.
rules.common_trap: There is no confinement if a reasonable means of escape exists and the plaintiff knows of it.

rules.title: Bounded Area
rules.prompt_question: What does confinement to a bounded area mean?
rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff must be confined within boundaries fixed by the defendant, and mere prevention from going in one direction is not enough if a reasonable escape route remains.
rules.application_example: Blocking one exit is not false imprisonment if another safe exit is open and known.
rules.common_trap: An escape route is not reasonable if it is hidden, dangerous, or humiliating.

rules.title: Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of IIED?
rules.rule_statement: IIED requires extreme and outrageous conduct that intentionally or recklessly causes the plaintiff severe emotional distress.
rules.application_example: Repeated outrageous harassment of a vulnerable person may support IIED.
rules.common_trap: Mere insults, rudeness, or indignities are usually not enough.

rules.title: Bystander IIED
rules.prompt_question: When may a bystander recover for IIED?
rules.rule_statement: A bystander may recover for IIED when the defendant intentionally or recklessly causes severe emotional distress through extreme and outrageous conduct directed at a close relative in the bystander's presence or where the defendant knows of the bystander's presence.
rules.application_example: A defendant who intentionally attacks a child in front of the parent may be liable for the parent's IIED.
rules.common_trap: This is not the same rule as negligent bystander emotional distress.

rules.title: Trespass to Land
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of trespass to land?
rules.rule_statement: Trespass to land requires an intentional physical invasion of the plaintiff's real property.
rules.application_example: Walking onto another's land without permission is trespass even if no damage occurs.
rules.common_trap: The defendant need only intend the entry, not intend wrongdoing.

rules.title: Trespass to Airspace and Subsurface
rules.prompt_question: When can interference with airspace or subsurface be trespass?
rules.rule_statement: A trespass may occur by intrusion into the immediate reaches of the landowner's airspace or into the subsurface if it interferes with possession.
rules.application_example: Blasting rocks onto neighboring land may be trespass.
rules.common_trap: Not every overflight is a trespass.

rules.title: Trespass to Chattels
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of trespass to chattels?
rules.rule_statement: Trespass to chattels requires an intentional interference with the plaintiff's right of possession in personal property that causes dispossession, impairment of condition, deprivation of use, or bodily harm to the possessor or something in which the possessor has a legally protected interest.
rules.application_example: Borrowing a phone without permission and draining its battery may be trespass to chattels.
rules.common_trap: Some actual harm or meaningful interference is generally required.

rules.title: Conversion
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of conversion?
rules.rule_statement: Conversion is an intentional exercise of dominion or control over the plaintiff's personal property that so seriously interferes with the right of possession that the defendant may be required to pay the full value of the property.
rules.application_example: Selling another person's bike as your own is conversion.
rules.common_trap: Conversion is a more serious interference than trespass to chattels.

rules.title: Seriousness of Interference in Conversion
rules.prompt_question: How is serious interference determined in conversion?
rules.rule_statement: Courts consider factors such as the extent and duration of dominion, the defendant's intent, good faith, harm done, and inconvenience caused to the plaintiff.
rules.application_example: A short accidental interference may be trespass to chattels rather than conversion.
rules.common_trap: The remedy usually tracks the seriousness of interference.

rules.title: Consent
rules.prompt_question: How does consent operate as a defense to intentional torts?
rules.rule_statement: Consent is a defense when the plaintiff had capacity and voluntarily agreed to the contact or invasion, and the defendant acted within the scope of that consent.
rules.application_example: A boxer consents to ordinary contacts within the match.
rules.common_trap: Consent obtained by fraud, duress, or incapacity may be invalid.

rules.title: Implied Consent
rules.prompt_question: When may consent be implied?
rules.rule_statement: Consent may be implied from custom, conduct, or surrounding circumstances when a reasonable person would understand the plaintiff to agree.
rules.application_example: Standing in line for a vaccination and extending an arm may imply consent to an ordinary injection.
rules.common_trap: Implied consent extends only to the contact reasonably contemplated.

rules.title: Self-Defense
rules.prompt_question: When may a defendant use self-defense?
rules.rule_statement: A person may use reasonable force to defend against an imminent harmful or offensive contact when the person reasonably believes force is necessary.
rules.application_example: A person may shove away an attacker who is about to strike.
rules.common_trap: Deadly force is permitted only when threatened with death or serious bodily harm.

rules.title: Retreat and Self-Defense
rules.prompt_question: Is retreat required before using deadly self-defense?
rules.rule_statement: Traditionally, retreat is not required before using reasonable nondeadly force, and many jurisdictions do not require retreat before deadly force if the person reasonably faces death or serious bodily harm, especially in the home.
rules.application_example: A homeowner usually need not retreat before defending against an intruder.
rules.common_trap: Jurisdictional variations exist, but MBE questions usually focus on reasonableness and imminent threat.

rules.title: Defense of Others
rules.prompt_question: When may a defendant defend others?
rules.rule_statement: A person may use reasonable force to defend another when the defender reasonably believes the other person is entitled to use self-defense.
rules.application_example: If A reasonably thinks B is about to be stabbed, A may intervene with reasonable force.
rules.common_trap: The force used must still be proportional.

rules.title: Defense of Property
rules.prompt_question: What force may be used to defend property?
rules.rule_statement: A person may use reasonable nondeadly force to prevent or terminate another's intrusion on land or chattels after a request to desist if feasible, but may not use deadly force solely to protect property.
rules.application_example: A landowner may physically remove a trespasser using reasonable force after warning if feasible.
rules.common_trap: Mechanical deadly devices are generally forbidden.

rules.title: Recapture of Chattels
rules.prompt_question: When may a person use force to recapture chattels?
rules.rule_statement: A person wrongfully dispossessed of chattels may use reasonable force in fresh pursuit after a timely demand for return, but not force if the property was lawfully acquired.
rules.application_example: A shop owner may chase a thief and use reasonable force to recover stolen goods.
rules.common_trap: There is no privilege to breach the peace after delay.

rules.title: Shopkeeper's Privilege
rules.prompt_question: What is the shopkeeper's privilege?
rules.rule_statement: A shopkeeper has a limited privilege to detain a suspected thief for a reasonable time in a reasonable manner when there is a reasonable belief theft occurred.
rules.application_example: A store may briefly stop a customer reasonably suspected of shoplifting while investigating.
rules.common_trap: Excessive force or prolonged detention destroys the privilege.

rules.title: Public Necessity
rules.prompt_question: What is the defense of public necessity?
rules.rule_statement: Public necessity is an absolute privilege to interfere with property when reasonably necessary to avert an imminent public disaster.
rules.application_example: Destroying a house to stop a citywide fire may be privileged.
rules.common_trap: The defendant generally owes no compensation under public necessity.

rules.title: Private Necessity
rules.prompt_question: What is the defense of private necessity?
rules.rule_statement: Private necessity is a qualified privilege to invade another's property to protect a person or property from serious harm, but the defendant remains liable for actual damage caused.
rules.application_example: Tying a boat to a private dock during a storm may be privileged, but the boat owner must pay for damage to the dock.
rules.common_trap: The landowner may not forcibly eject the defendant while the necessity continues.

rules.title: Parental Discipline
rules.prompt_question: What privilege does a parent have to discipline a child?
rules.rule_statement: A parent or one in loco parentis may use reasonable force to discipline a child when reasonably necessary.
rules.application_example: Moderate physical discipline may be privileged if reasonable under the circumstances.
rules.common_trap: Excessive force is not privileged.

rules.title: General Duty of Care
rules.prompt_question: What is the general duty of care in negligence?
rules.rule_statement: A defendant generally owes a duty to exercise the care that a reasonably prudent person would use under like circumstances to avoid foreseeable risks of harm to foreseeable plaintiffs.
rules.application_example: Drivers must use ordinary reasonable care when operating a vehicle.
rules.common_trap: Duty is usually objective unless a special rule applies.

rules.title: Foreseeable Plaintiffs
rules.prompt_question: To whom is a duty owed in negligence?
rules.rule_statement: A duty is ordinarily owed only to those plaintiffs who are within the zone of foreseeable risk created by the defendant's conduct.
rules.application_example: A railroad employee who negligently jostles a passenger may owe a duty to nearby persons foreseeably endangered, not to unforeseeable distant plaintiffs.
rules.common_trap: This issue often appears in proximate-cause flavored fact patterns but is framed as duty.

rules.title: No General Duty to Rescue
rules.prompt_question: Is there a general duty to rescue?
rules.rule_statement: There is generally no duty to rescue or aid another absent a special relationship, statutory duty, voluntary undertaking, or creation of the peril.
rules.application_example: A bystander usually has no duty to jump into a river to save a stranger.
rules.common_trap: Once a rescue is undertaken, reasonable care is required.

rules.title: Duty When Defendant Creates Peril
rules.prompt_question: What duty arises when the defendant creates a peril?
rules.rule_statement: A defendant who creates a risk of harm, even innocently, generally has a duty to take reasonable steps to prevent or reduce the harm.
rules.application_example: A driver who accidentally knocks down a sign in the road may have a duty to warn others.
rules.common_trap: The duty can arise even if the initial conduct was not tortious.

rules.title: Special Relationships
rules.prompt_question: When does a special relationship create an affirmative duty?
rules.rule_statement: Special relationships such as common carrier-passenger, innkeeper-guest, business-invitee, school-student, employer-employee, or custodian-dependent may create a duty to aid, protect, or control.
rules.application_example: A hotel may owe guests a duty to take reasonable precautions against foreseeable criminal attacks.
rules.common_trap: The relationship must be recognized and the harm must often be foreseeable.

rules.title: Duty to Control Third Parties
rules.prompt_question: When is there a duty to control the conduct of third parties?
rules.rule_statement: A duty to control the conduct of a third party may arise when the defendant has a special relationship with either the third party or the potential victim and the harm is foreseeable.
rules.application_example: A mental health professional may in some circumstances have a duty to warn an identifiable victim of a serious threat.
rules.common_trap: There is no broad general duty to control others absent a recognized basis.

rules.title: Pure Economic Loss
rules.prompt_question: Can a plaintiff recover for pure economic loss in negligence?
rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff generally may not recover in ordinary negligence for purely economic loss unaccompanied by personal injury or property damage, absent a special relationship or recognized exception.
rules.application_example: A business usually cannot recover lost profits solely because a nearby bridge was negligently damaged and traffic decreased.
rules.common_trap: Misrepresentation and professional-duty cases may be treated differently.

rules.title: Emotional Distress Without Physical Harm
rules.prompt_question: When are claims for emotional distress allowed without physical impact?
rules.rule_statement: Claims for emotional distress without physical harm are limited and generally require a recognized theory such as zone-of-danger or bystander recovery, depending on jurisdiction.
rules.application_example: A plaintiff almost hit by a car may recover emotional distress damages in some jurisdictions.
rules.common_trap: This is an area of jurisdictional variation.

rules.title: Duty to Trespassers
rules.prompt_question: What duty does a landowner owe trespassers?
rules.rule_statement: A landowner generally owes an undiscovered trespasser only a duty to refrain from willful, wanton, or intentional misconduct, but owes a known or anticipated trespasser a duty to warn of known artificial hidden dangerous conditions.
rules.application_example: If a landowner knows people cut across land, a hidden electrified wire may require warning.
rules.common_trap: There is generally no duty to inspect for trespassers.

rules.title: Duty to Licensees
rules.prompt_question: What duty does a landowner owe licensees?
rules.rule_statement: A landowner owes a licensee a duty to warn of known dangerous conditions that the licensee is unlikely to discover.
rules.application_example: A host should warn a social guest about a hidden weak stair the host knows about.
rules.common_trap: Traditionally there is no duty to inspect for a licensee's benefit.

rules.title: Duty to Invitees
rules.prompt_question: What duty does a landowner owe invitees?
rules.rule_statement: A landowner owes an invitee a duty to use reasonable care to protect against known dangers and those that would be revealed by reasonable inspection.
rules.application_example: A grocery store must inspect aisles for spills and either clean them or warn customers.
rules.common_trap: Invitees receive the greatest protection under traditional entrant rules.

rules.title: Attractive Nuisance
rules.prompt_question: When may a landowner be liable to child trespassers under the attractive nuisance doctrine?
rules.rule_statement: A landowner may be liable for an artificial condition highly dangerous to child trespassers if children are likely to trespass, the condition poses an unreasonable risk, children cannot appreciate the danger because of youth, the burden of eliminating the danger is slight compared with the risk, and the landowner fails to use reasonable care.
rules.application_example: An unfenced pool may support attractive nuisance liability.
rules.common_trap: The doctrine generally applies only to artificial, not natural, conditions.

rules.title: Landlord Liability
rules.prompt_question: When may a landlord be liable in tort to tenants or others?
rules.rule_statement: A landlord may be liable for common areas under landlord control, hidden defects known at lease inception, negligent repairs, and defects existing at the time of lease for public use.
rules.application_example: A landlord may be liable for injuries on a poorly maintained common stairwell.
rules.common_trap: Once possession passes to the tenant, landlord liability is limited to recognized exceptions.

rules.title: Recreational Use Protection
rules.prompt_question: What is the effect of recreational use statutes?
rules.rule_statement: Recreational use statutes often limit landowner liability when land is opened for public recreational use without charge, except for willful or malicious conduct.
rules.application_example: A landowner who allows free hiking may receive statutory protection from ordinary-negligence claims.
rules.common_trap: The protection is statutory and may not apply if admission is charged.

rules.title: Reasonably Prudent Person
rules.prompt_question: What is the ordinary negligence standard of care?
rules.rule_statement: The ordinary standard of care is that of a reasonably prudent person acting under the same or similar circumstances.
rules.application_example: A driver must react as a reasonable driver would to wet pavement.
rules.common_trap: The standard is objective.

rules.title: Children
rules.prompt_question: What standard of care applies to children?
rules.rule_statement: A child is held to the care of a reasonably careful child of the same age, intelligence, and experience, unless the child is engaged in an adult activity.
rules.application_example: A child bicyclist is judged by the child standard, but a child driver by the adult standard.
rules.common_trap: Adult activities trigger the adult standard.

rules.title: Physical Disabilities
rules.prompt_question: How do physical disabilities affect the standard of care?
rules.rule_statement: A person with a physical disability is held to the standard of a reasonably careful person with the same disability.
keywords: reasonably careful person with the same disability
rules.application_example: A blind pedestrian is judged by the care of a reasonable blind person.
rules.common_trap: The law adjusts for physical but generally not sudden voluntary impairment.

rules.title: Mental Disabilities
rules.prompt_question: How do mental disabilities affect the standard of care?
rules.rule_statement: A person with a mental disability is generally held to the ordinary reasonable person standard.
rules.application_example: A defendant's poor judgment from mental illness usually does not lower the negligence standard.
rules.common_trap: This differs from the rule for physical disabilities.

rules.title: Professionals
rules.prompt_question: What standard applies to professionals?
rules.rule_statement: Professionals must exercise the knowledge, skill, and care of a reasonably prudent member of the profession in similar circumstances.
rules.application_example: A physician is judged by the professional standard rather than the ordinary person standard.
rules.common_trap: Expert testimony is usually needed, except for obvious negligence.

rules.title: Common Carriers and Innkeepers
rules.prompt_question: What special duty applies to common carriers and innkeepers?
rules.rule_statement: Common carriers and innkeepers traditionally owe a high degree of care to passengers and guests, respectively.
rules.application_example: A bus line must exercise great care for passenger safety.
rules.common_trap: Special duty does not eliminate the need to prove causation and damages.

rules.title: Custom
rules.prompt_question: What role does custom play in negligence?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence of custom is relevant to breach but is not conclusive because reasonable care may require more or less than custom.
rules.application_example: Industry practice may be evidence of due care, but an entire industry may still be negligent.
rules.common_trap: Custom is evidence, not the ultimate test.

rules.title: Negligence Per Se
rules.prompt_question: When does negligence per se apply?
rules.rule_statement: Negligence per se applies when the defendant violates a statute, the plaintiff is within the class the statute protects, and the harm is of the type the statute seeks to prevent.
rules.application_example: Running a stop sign may be negligence per se toward another motorist injured in the collision.
rules.common_trap: A valid excuse may defeat negligence per se.

rules.title: Excuse to Statutory Violation
rules.prompt_question: When may a statutory violation be excused?
rules.rule_statement: A statutory violation may be excused when compliance would be more dangerous, impossible, or beyond the actor's knowledge despite reasonable care, or where another recognized excuse applies.
rules.application_example: Crossing the center line to avoid a child in the road may excuse a traffic violation.
rules.common_trap: Do not assume every violation automatically establishes negligence.

rules.title: Breach
rules.prompt_question: How is breach established in negligence?
rules.rule_statement: Breach is established when the defendant fails to act as a reasonably prudent person would under the circumstances.
rules.application_example: Driving too fast in a school zone may breach the duty of care.
rules.common_trap: Breach is separate from causation and damages.

rules.title: Risk-Utility and Hand Formula
rules.prompt_question: How may courts evaluate breach using risk and burden?
rules.rule_statement: Breach may be assessed by balancing the burden of precautions against the probability and gravity of harm, as reflected in the Hand formula.
rules.application_example: Installing an inexpensive guard against a serious foreseeable injury may be required.
rules.common_trap: The formula is conceptual and does not replace ordinary reasonableness analysis.

rules.title: Res Ipsa Loquitur
rules.prompt_question: When does res ipsa loquitur apply?
rules.rule_statement: Res ipsa loquitur permits an inference of negligence when the event ordinarily does not occur absent negligence, the instrumentality was under the defendant's exclusive control, and the plaintiff did not contribute to the harm.
keywords: ordinarily does not occur absent negligence
rules.application_example: A surgical sponge left inside a patient may support an inference of negligence.
rules.common_trap: It permits but does not compel a finding of negligence.

rules.title: Actual Cause: But For
rules.prompt_question: What is actual cause in negligence?
rules.rule_statement: Actual cause exists when the plaintiff's injury would not have occurred but for the defendant's breach.
rules.application_example: If the collision would not have occurred absent the defendant's speeding, actual cause is satisfied.
rules.common_trap: Multiple sufficient causes may require a different formulation.

rules.title: Multiple Sufficient Causes
rules.prompt_question: How is actual cause shown when multiple sufficient causes exist?
rules.rule_statement: When two or more independent acts each would have been sufficient to cause the harm, each act is an actual cause if it was a substantial factor in bringing about the harm.
rules.application_example: If two separate fires each would have burned the same house, each negligent fire setter may be an actual cause.
rules.common_trap: Do not use but-for strictly in overdetermined-cause cases.

rules.title: Loss of Chance
rules.prompt_question: What is the loss-of-chance doctrine?
rules.rule_statement: Some jurisdictions allow a medical malpractice plaintiff to recover for the loss of a substantial chance of a better outcome caused by negligence.
rules.application_example: Failure to diagnose cancer that reduces survival chances may support recovery in some jurisdictions.
rules.common_trap: This is jurisdiction-specific and not universal.

rules.title: Proximate Cause
rules.prompt_question: What is proximate cause?
rules.rule_statement: Proximate cause limits liability to those harms that are within the scope of the foreseeable risk created by the defendant's conduct.
rules.application_example: Negligent driving foreseeably causes collision-related injuries.
rules.common_trap: Highly extraordinary consequences may be too remote.

rules.title: Superseding Cause
rules.prompt_question: When does an intervening cause become a superseding cause?
rules.rule_statement: An intervening cause becomes superseding when it is so unforeseeable that it breaks the chain of proximate causation and relieves the defendant of liability for subsequent harm.
rules.application_example: A bizarre criminal act far outside the risk created by the defendant may be superseding.
rules.common_trap: Foreseeable intervening acts usually do not cut off liability.

rules.title: Rescue Doctrine
rules.prompt_question: How does the rescue doctrine affect liability?
rules.rule_statement: If the defendant's negligence imperils a victim, the defendant is generally liable for injuries suffered by a foreseeable rescuer unless the rescuer acts rashly or recklessly.
rules.application_example: A negligent driver may be liable when a rescuer is injured pulling passengers from the wreck.
rules.common_trap: Danger invites rescue.

rules.title: Eggshell Plaintiff Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the eggshell plaintiff rule?
rules.rule_statement: A defendant takes the plaintiff as found and is liable for the full extent of harm even if the plaintiff's unusual fragility makes the injury worse than expected.
rules.application_example: A slight blow causing severe injury because of a latent condition still results in full liability.
rules.common_trap: Foreseeability of the extent of harm is not required once some personal injury is foreseeable.

rules.title: Actual Harm
rules.prompt_question: What damages are required for negligence?
rules.rule_statement: Negligence requires actual legally cognizable harm or damage.
rules.application_example: A close call without injury or loss is not actionable negligence.
rules.common_trap: Nominal damages are not enough for ordinary negligence.

rules.title: Personal Injury Damages
rules.prompt_question: What damages are recoverable for personal injury in negligence?
rules.rule_statement: A prevailing plaintiff may recover past and future medical expenses, lost earnings or earning capacity, pain and suffering, and property damage caused by the tort.
rules.application_example: An injured plaintiff may recover both past bills and reasonably certain future treatment costs.
rules.common_trap: Future damages must be proven with reasonable certainty.

rules.title: Wrongful Death
rules.prompt_question: What is a wrongful death action?
rules.rule_statement: A wrongful death action is a statutory claim brought by designated survivors for their own losses resulting from the decedent's death caused by the defendant's tort.
rules.application_example: A spouse may recover lost financial support after wrongful death.
rules.common_trap: This differs from a survival action.

rules.title: Survival Action
rules.prompt_question: What is a survival action?
rules.rule_statement: A survival action preserves the decedent's own claim and allows the estate to recover damages the decedent sustained before death.
rules.application_example: The estate may recover the decedent's medical expenses and pain before death.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse survival damages with survivors' wrongful-death losses.

rules.title: Negligent Infliction of Emotional Distress: Zone of Danger
rules.prompt_question: When may a plaintiff recover for NIED under the zone-of-danger rule?
rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff may recover for negligent infliction of emotional distress when placed in immediate risk of physical harm by the defendant's negligence and emotional distress results, often with required physical manifestations depending on jurisdiction.
rules.application_example: A person nearly struck by a negligently driven car may recover in some jurisdictions.
rules.common_trap: Jurisdictions vary on physical-symptom requirements.

rules.title: Negligent Infliction of Emotional Distress: Bystander
rules.prompt_question: When may a bystander recover for NIED?
rules.rule_statement: A bystander may recover in many jurisdictions when closely related to the injured victim, present at the scene, and contemporaneously aware of the injury-producing event.
rules.application_example: A parent who sees a child struck by a car may recover emotional distress damages in many jurisdictions.
rules.common_trap: Learning of the injury later is usually insufficient.

rules.title: Contributory Negligence
rules.prompt_question: What is contributory negligence?
rules.rule_statement: Under pure contributory negligence, any negligence by the plaintiff that contributes to the injury completely bars recovery, subject to limited exceptions such as last clear chance.
rules.application_example: A plaintiff who carelessly steps into traffic may be barred entirely in a contributory-negligence jurisdiction.
rules.common_trap: Most jurisdictions instead use comparative negligence.

rules.title: Last Clear Chance
rules.prompt_question: What is the last clear chance doctrine?
rules.rule_statement: Under the last clear chance doctrine, a contributorily negligent plaintiff may still recover if the defendant had the last clear opportunity to avoid the harm but failed to do so.
rules.application_example: A driver who sees a careless pedestrian in time to stop but does not may still be liable.
rules.common_trap: This doctrine is mainly relevant in contributory negligence jurisdictions.

rules.title: Pure Comparative Negligence
rules.prompt_question: What is pure comparative negligence?
rules.rule_statement: Under pure comparative negligence, the plaintiff's recovery is reduced by the percentage of the plaintiff's fault.
keywords: reduced by the percentage of fault
rules.application_example: A plaintiff 40 percent at fault recovers 60 percent of total damages.
rules.common_trap: The plaintiff may recover even if more at fault than the defendant.

rules.title: Modified Comparative Negligence
rules.prompt_question: What is modified comparative negligence?
rules.rule_statement: Under modified comparative negligence, the plaintiff's recovery is reduced by fault but barred if the plaintiff's fault reaches or exceeds the jurisdiction's threshold, typically 50 percent or 51 percent.
rules.application_example: A plaintiff 51 percent at fault may be barred in some jurisdictions.
rules.common_trap: Know whether the bar is 50 percent or greater than 50 percent.

rules.title: Assumption of Risk
rules.prompt_question: What is assumption of risk?
rules.rule_statement: Assumption of risk applies when the plaintiff knew of a specific risk and voluntarily encountered it.
rules.application_example: A spectator who knowingly sits in an unprotected baseball area may assume the inherent risk of foul balls.
rules.common_trap: General awareness of danger is not always enough.

rules.title: Avoidable Consequences
rules.prompt_question: What is the avoidable-consequences rule in tort damages?
rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff may not recover damages that reasonably could have been avoided after the tort occurred.
rules.application_example: A plaintiff who unreasonably refuses needed medical treatment may lose recovery for aggravated harm.
rules.common_trap: This is post-injury mitigation, not comparative fault before the accident.

rules.title: Respondeat Superior
rules.prompt_question: When is an employer liable under respondeat superior?
rules.rule_statement: An employer is vicariously liable for torts committed by an employee acting within the scope of employment.
rules.application_example: A delivery company may be liable when its driver negligently causes a crash while making deliveries.
rules.common_trap: Intentional torts are usually outside scope unless job-related or foreseeable in the employment.

rules.title: Scope of Employment
rules.prompt_question: When does conduct fall within the scope of employment?
rules.rule_statement: Conduct falls within the scope of employment when it is of the kind the employee was hired to perform, occurs substantially within authorized time and space limits, and is motivated at least in part to serve the employer.
rules.application_example: A delivery driver's negligent driving during deliveries is within scope.
rules.common_trap: A frolic is outside scope, while a mere detour may still be within scope.

rules.title: Independent Contractors
rules.prompt_question: Is a principal generally liable for the torts of an independent contractor?
rules.rule_statement: A principal is generally not vicariously liable for the torts of an independent contractor, subject to exceptions such as nondelegable duties, inherently dangerous activities, and negligent hiring.
rules.application_example: A business hiring an outside roofer usually is not automatically liable for the roofer's negligence.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse independent contractors with employees.

rules.title: Nondelegable Duties
rules.prompt_question: What is a nondelegable duty?
rules.rule_statement: A nondelegable duty is one that the defendant cannot avoid by hiring an independent contractor, so the defendant remains liable for negligence in performing that duty.
rules.application_example: A land possessor may remain liable for certain duties to entrants even if maintenance is outsourced.
rules.common_trap: This is a key exception to the independent-contractor rule.

rules.title: Joint and Several Liability
rules.prompt_question: What is joint and several liability?
rules.rule_statement: Under joint and several liability, each liable defendant may be responsible for the entire indivisible harm, subject to contribution or indemnity rights.
rules.application_example: If two negligent drivers cause one indivisible injury, the plaintiff may recover the full amount from either one in jurisdictions retaining the doctrine.
rules.common_trap: Some jurisdictions modify or abolish it for comparative fault.

rules.title: Contribution
rules.prompt_question: What is contribution among tortfeasors?
rules.rule_statement: Contribution allows a tortfeasor who paid more than a fair share of liability to recover the excess from other jointly liable tortfeasors.
keywords: paid more than a fair share
rules.application_example: A defendant who pays the entire judgment may seek partial reimbursement from another liable defendant.
rules.common_trap: Contribution differs from indemnity, which shifts the entire loss.

rules.title: Indemnity
rules.prompt_question: What is indemnity?
rules.rule_statement: Indemnity shifts the entire loss from one tortfeasor to another when law or equity requires full reimbursement, such as where liability is purely vicarious.
rules.application_example: An employer held vicariously liable may seek indemnity from the employee who actually committed the tort.
rules.common_trap: Indemnity is full shifting, not partial sharing.

rules.title: Wild Animals
rules.prompt_question: When is a possessor of a wild animal strictly liable?
rules.rule_statement: A possessor of a wild animal is strictly liable for harm caused by the animal's dangerous propensities.
rules.application_example: A zoo visitor injured by an escaped tiger may recover under strict liability.
rules.common_trap: The harm must result from the dangerous propensity that makes the animal wild.

rules.title: Domestic Animals with Known Dangerous Propensities
rules.prompt_question: When is there strict liability for domestic animals?
rules.rule_statement: The owner of a domestic animal is strictly liable if the owner knew or should have known of the animal's dangerous propensities abnormal to its class.
rules.application_example: An owner who knows a dog has vicious tendencies may be strictly liable for another bite.
rules.common_trap: Ordinary animal behavior is not enough.

rules.title: Abnormally Dangerous Activities
rules.prompt_question: When is a defendant strictly liable for an abnormally dangerous activity?
rules.rule_statement: A defendant is strictly liable for harm resulting from an abnormally dangerous activity that creates a foreseeable and highly significant risk of serious harm even when reasonable care is used and is not a matter of common usage.
rules.application_example: Blasting with dynamite is the classic example.
rules.common_trap: Strict liability is limited to the kind of harm that makes the activity abnormally dangerous.

rules.title: Defenses to Abnormally Dangerous Activity Claims
rules.prompt_question: What defenses may apply to strict liability for abnormally dangerous activities?
rules.rule_statement: Assumption of risk is a defense to strict liability, while ordinary contributory negligence usually is not unless the plaintiff knowingly and unreasonably encountered the risk.
rules.application_example: A plaintiff who deliberately enters a blasting zone may be barred or reduced depending on doctrine used.
rules.common_trap: Do not import ordinary negligence defenses mechanically into strict liability.

rules.title: Strict Products Liability
rules.prompt_question: What must a plaintiff prove in strict products liability?
rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff must show the defendant is a commercial supplier, the product was defective, the defect existed when it left the defendant's control, and the defect actually and proximately caused the plaintiff's injury while the product was being used in a foreseeable manner.
rules.application_example: A retailer may be strictly liable for selling a defectively manufactured toaster that injures a consumer.
rules.common_trap: Privity is not required in modern strict products liability.

rules.title: Manufacturing Defect
rules.prompt_question: What is a manufacturing defect?
rules.rule_statement: A manufacturing defect exists when a product departs from its intended design and is more dangerous than consumers expect.
rules.application_example: A cracked soda bottle that explodes because it was improperly made has a manufacturing defect.
rules.common_trap: The whole product line need not be defective.

rules.title: Design Defect
rules.prompt_question: What is a design defect?
rules.rule_statement: A design defect exists when the product design creates a foreseeable risk of harm that could have been reduced or avoided by a reasonable alternative design and the failure to adopt that design renders the product not reasonably safe.
rules.application_example: A vehicle design that creates unnecessary rollover risk may be defective.
rules.common_trap: The reasonable alternative design requirement is central on exams.

rules.title: Failure to Warn
rules.prompt_question: When is a product defective for failure to warn?
rules.rule_statement: A product is defective for failure to warn when foreseeable risks of harm could have been reduced or avoided by reasonable instructions or warnings and the omission renders the product not reasonably safe.
rules.application_example: A prescription drug lacking adequate warning of known severe side effects may be defectively marketed.
rules.common_trap: There is generally no duty to warn of open and obvious dangers.

rules.title: Commercial Suppliers
rules.prompt_question: Who may be strictly liable for defective products?
rules.rule_statement: All commercial suppliers in the distribution chain, including manufacturers, wholesalers, and retailers, may be strictly liable for defective products.
rules.application_example: An injured consumer may sue both the manufacturer and retailer.
rules.common_trap: Casual sellers are generally not strictly liable.

rules.title: Products Causation
rules.prompt_question: What causation must be shown in products liability?
rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff must prove the product defect actually and proximately caused the injury.
rules.application_example: A defectively designed ladder must be shown to have caused the fall.
rules.common_trap: Misuse may affect causation or comparative fault.

rules.title: Product Misuse
rules.prompt_question: How does product misuse affect liability?
rules.rule_statement: Unforeseeable misuse of a product may defeat or reduce products liability, while foreseeable misuse may still support liability.
rules.application_example: Using a chair as a normal step stool might be foreseeable, but using it as a moving vehicle may not be.
rules.common_trap: Misuse is not automatically a defense if the misuse was foreseeable.

rules.title: Assumption of Risk in Products Cases
rules.prompt_question: How does assumption of risk operate in products liability?
rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff who discovers a product defect and voluntarily proceeds to use the product despite the known danger may be barred or have recovery reduced depending on the jurisdiction's fault system.
rules.application_example: Continuing to use a visibly broken machine guard after appreciating the danger may invoke assumption of risk.
rules.common_trap: Mere failure to inspect for defects is usually not enough.

rules.title: Express Warranty
rules.prompt_question: How is an express warranty created?
rules.rule_statement: An express warranty is created by an affirmation of fact, promise, description, or sample that becomes part of the basis of the bargain.
rules.application_example: A seller's statement that a saw cuts steel may create an express warranty.
rules.common_trap: Puffery usually does not create an express warranty.

rules.title: Implied Warranty of Merchantability
rules.prompt_question: When does the implied warranty of merchantability arise?
rules.rule_statement: The implied warranty of merchantability arises when a merchant sells goods and warrants that they are fit for ordinary purposes.
rules.application_example: A merchant seller of food warrants the food is fit to eat.
rules.common_trap: A casual seller usually does not give this warranty.

rules.title: Implied Warranty of Fitness for Particular Purpose
rules.prompt_question: When does the implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose arise?
rules.rule_statement: This warranty arises when the seller has reason to know the buyer's particular purpose and that the buyer is relying on the seller's skill or judgment to select suitable goods.
keywords: relying on seller's skill or judgment
rules.application_example: A buyer who tells the seller paint is needed for marine use and relies on the seller's recommendation may receive this warranty.
rules.common_trap: The purpose must be particular, not merely ordinary.

rules.title: Private Nuisance
rules.prompt_question: What is private nuisance?
rules.rule_statement: Private nuisance is a substantial and unreasonable interference with the plaintiff's use and enjoyment of land.
rules.application_example: Loud constant vibrations and fumes from neighboring land may constitute private nuisance.
rules.common_trap: The plaintiff must have a possessory interest in the land.

rules.title: Public Nuisance
rules.prompt_question: What is public nuisance?
rules.rule_statement: Public nuisance is an unreasonable interference with a right common to the general public.
keywords: right common to the general public
rules.application_example: Blocking a public highway may be a public nuisance.
rules.common_trap: A private plaintiff must usually show special harm different in kind from that suffered by the general public.

rules.title: Remedies for Nuisance
rules.prompt_question: What remedies are available for nuisance?
rules.rule_statement: Remedies for nuisance may include damages, injunction, or abatement, depending on the balance of hardships and adequacy of legal relief.
rules.application_example: A court may award damages instead of an injunction where shutting down an activity would impose extreme hardship.
rules.common_trap: Equitable relief depends on more than proving the nuisance itself.

rules.title: Defamation
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of defamation?
rules.rule_statement: Defamation requires a defamatory statement of fact concerning the plaintiff, publication to a third person, fault by the defendant, falsity where required, and damages unless the statement is actionable per se.
rules.application_example: Falsely accusing someone of embezzlement to coworkers may be defamation.
rules.common_trap: Pure opinion is generally not actionable.

rules.title: Defamation Per Se
rules.prompt_question: What statements are defamatory per se?
rules.rule_statement: Statements are traditionally defamatory per se if they impute serious crime, loathsome disease, unchastity, or conduct adversely affecting business, trade, profession, or office.
rules.application_example: Falsely stating that a doctor is incompetent may be defamation per se.
rules.common_trap: Per se categories may affect presumed damages at common law, subject to constitutional limits.

rules.title: Truth as Defense
rules.prompt_question: Is truth a defense to defamation?
rules.rule_statement: Truth is an absolute defense to defamation.
rules.application_example: A true report that the plaintiff was convicted defeats a defamation claim.
rules.common_trap: Substantial truth is generally enough.

rules.title: Privileges in Defamation
rules.prompt_question: What privileges may defeat a defamation claim?
rules.rule_statement: Absolute privileges protect statements in judicial, legislative, and certain executive proceedings, while qualified privileges protect statements made in good faith on proper occasions to proper recipients.
rules.application_example: Statements in court pleadings are generally absolutely privileged.
rules.common_trap: Qualified privilege is lost by abuse, including malice or excessive publication.

rules.title: Public Officials and Public Figures
rules.prompt_question: What must a public official or public figure prove in defamation?
rules.rule_statement: A public official or public figure must prove by clear and convincing evidence that the defendant acted with actual malice, meaning knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for truth.
rules.application_example: A celebrity suing over a false news article must prove actual malice.
rules.common_trap: Actual malice concerns awareness of falsity, not spite.

rules.title: Private Figures
rules.prompt_question: What fault standard applies to private figures in defamation?
rules.rule_statement: A private figure generally must prove at least negligence regarding falsity, and actual malice is required for presumed or punitive damages involving matters of public concern.
rules.application_example: A private citizen defamed by a newspaper usually need not prove actual malice to recover actual damages.
rules.common_trap: The public-concern distinction affects damages.

rules.title: Intrusion Upon Seclusion
rules.prompt_question: What is intrusion upon seclusion?
rules.rule_statement: Intrusion upon seclusion is an intentional intrusion into the plaintiff's private affairs in a manner highly offensive to a reasonable person.
rules.application_example: Secretly recording someone in a bedroom may constitute intrusion.
rules.common_trap: Publication is not required.

rules.title: Public Disclosure of Private Facts
rules.prompt_question: What is public disclosure of private facts?
rules.rule_statement: This tort requires public disclosure of private information about the plaintiff that would be highly offensive to a reasonable person and is not of legitimate public concern.
rules.application_example: Publicizing a person's private medical records may create liability.
rules.common_trap: Truth is not a defense to this privacy tort.

rules.title: False Light
rules.prompt_question: What is false light?
rules.rule_statement: False light occurs when the defendant publicizes matter placing the plaintiff in a false light that would be highly offensive to a reasonable person, subject to constitutional limits in matters of public concern.
rules.application_example: Publishing a story falsely implying someone sympathizes with terrorists may place the person in a false light.
rules.common_trap: False light overlaps with defamation but is distinct.

rules.title: Appropriation
rules.prompt_question: What is appropriation?
rules.rule_statement: Appropriation is the unauthorized use of the plaintiff's name or likeness for the defendant's commercial advantage.
rules.application_example: Using an athlete's photo in an advertisement without permission is appropriation.
rules.common_trap: News reporting and other protected uses are treated differently.

rules.title: Fraudulent Misrepresentation
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of fraudulent misrepresentation?
rules.rule_statement: Fraudulent misrepresentation requires a material misrepresentation of present or past fact made with scienter, intent to induce reliance, actual justifiable reliance, and damages.
rules.application_example: A seller who knowingly lies about a car's accident history to induce a sale may be liable for fraud.
rules.common_trap: Promises of future intent are usually not fraud unless the speaker had no intent to perform when speaking.

rules.title: Negligent Misrepresentation
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of negligent misrepresentation?
rules.rule_statement: Negligent misrepresentation requires a misrepresentation made without reasonable care, justifiable reliance by the plaintiff, and pecuniary loss, often in a business or professional context.
rules.application_example: An accountant carelessly preparing a financial statement for a known limited group of users may be liable to a relying lender.
rules.common_trap: Liability is often limited to foreseeable or known users of the information.

rules.title: Innocent Misrepresentation
rules.prompt_question: Can innocent misrepresentation create tort liability?
rules.rule_statement: Innocent misrepresentation generally does not support common law deceit damages, though it may justify rescission in contract and may trigger liability in limited contexts such as warranties.
rules.application_example: A seller's honest but false statement may support contract rescission but not fraud damages.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse tort fraud with contract remedies.

rules.title: Intentional Interference with Contract
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of intentional interference with contract?
rules.rule_statement: This tort requires a valid contract, defendant's knowledge of the contract, intentional inducement of breach, actual breach or disruption, and resulting damages.
rules.application_example: A competitor who knowingly persuades a singer to break an exclusive contract may be liable.
rules.common_trap: The contract must be valid and existing.

rules.title: Intentional Interference with Business Expectancy
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of intentional interference with business expectancy?
rules.rule_statement: This tort requires a valid business expectancy, defendant's knowledge, intentional interference by improper means or improper purpose, causation, and damages.
rules.application_example: Using fraud to sabotage another's likely contract may create liability.
rules.common_trap: A mere hope of future business is not enough without a concrete expectancy.
